title: UPS Room Sizing Guide
summary: Understand UPS cabinet count, battery cabinet count, cabinet footprint, clearance multiplier, and support-room area.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the planning area needed for UPS and battery cabinets after cabinet count, footprint, and clearance factor are included.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning UPS rooms, battery spaces, electrical rooms, equipment closets, or support areas where service clearance and cabinet footprint matter.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the UPS Room Sizing tool and explain how each value affects support-space requirements.

items.label: UPS Cabinets
items.description: The number of UPS cabinets included in the room or support area.
items.whyItMatters: UPS cabinet count drives base footprint and service clearance needs.
items.commonMistake: Counting the UPS load but forgetting the physical cabinet footprint.

items.label: Battery Cabinets
items.description: The number of battery cabinets or battery strings requiring floor space.
items.whyItMatters: Battery cabinets often consume as much or more planning space than the UPS cabinet.
items.commonMistake: Leaving no space for additional battery cabinets or replacement access.

items.label: Area per Cabinet (sq ft)
items.description: The estimated floor area for each UPS or battery cabinet before clearance factor is applied.
items.whyItMatters: The cabinet area is the base footprint for the room sizing estimate.
items.commonMistake: Using approximate area when the selected UPS model has specific footprint and clearance requirements.

items.label: Clearance Factor
items.description: A multiplier that accounts for service access, code working clearance, ventilation, door swing, and maintenance space around cabinets.
items.whyItMatters: UPS and battery systems require usable working space, not just enough room to physically fit the cabinets.
items.commonMistake: Treating clearance as optional because the cabinets fit on paper.

links.label: Check runtime assumptions
links.note: Useful when UPS space is part of a broader backup-power plan.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the UPS support space appears workable. Watch means clearance or expansion should be reviewed. Risk means the room may be too tight for safe service or future battery needs.
bullets: Healthy: cabinet footprint and clearance have usable margin.
Watch: verify manufacturer clearances, battery access, ventilation, and code working space.
Risk: increase room area, reduce equipment, or revise the support-room layout.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
